q: What's the difference between monitoring and observability?
a: Monitoring watches known signals against predefined thresholds (known failure modes). Observability is the broader ability to understand a system's internal state from its outputs well enough to debug novel, unanticipated problems. Monitoring is part of observability.

q: What are the three pillars of observability and what does each answer best?
a: Metrics (is something wrong? overall trend — cheap, always-on), Logs (what exactly happened in this event? detailed context), Traces (where across services did the time/error occur?). They're most powerful when correlated.

q: Why is correlation between the pillars so valuable?
a: It lets you pivot during an incident: a metric spikes → jump to traces to localize the service/operation → jump to that operation's logs (via shared trace_id) for the exact error. Detect → localize → explain.

q: What is OpenTelemetry?
a: A vendor-neutral CNCF standard (APIs, SDKs, Collector) for telemetry across traces, metrics, and logs. Instrument once with OTel and export to any compatible backend — avoiding vendor lock-in.

q: What is a metric, and what do labels add?
a: A numeric measurement over time (a time series of timestamp/value), identified by a name plus labels (key/value dimensions). Labels let you slice and aggregate one metric many ways (by service, status, route).

q: Why are high-cardinality labels dangerous?
a: Each unique label combination is a separate time series. Putting unbounded values (user IDs, request IDs, raw URLs) in labels can explode into millions of series and overwhelm the metrics store. Keep label values bounded.
hint: Don't put user IDs in labels.

q: What's the difference between a counter and a gauge?
a: A counter only goes up (or resets to 0 on restart) — total requests/errors; you read its rate, not the raw value. A gauge goes up and down — current memory, queue depth, in-flight requests.

q: What does a histogram metric enable?
a: It buckets observations (e.g. request durations) so you can compute percentiles/quantiles (p50, p95, p99) at query time — e.g. histogram_quantile(0.99, ...). Crucial because tail latency matters more than averages.

q: Why does Prometheus use a pull (scrape) model, and what's the benefit?
a: Prometheus scrapes targets' /metrics endpoints on its schedule. Benefits: it controls timing, a failed scrape itself signals a down target, no app-side buffering, and service discovery (esp. in Kubernetes) auto-finds new pods to scrape.

q: In PromQL, why do you graph rate(counter[5m]) instead of the counter itself?
a: A counter's raw value is an ever-growing (and reset-prone) number that's meaningless to graph. rate(counter[5m]) gives the per-second rate over the window and correctly handles restarts. Aggregate across series with sum by (label).

q: Why log structured (JSON) instead of free text?
a: Structured logs are queryable by field (e.g. level=error AND service=payments AND retries>2) instead of brittle regex grepping. At scale this means answering questions in seconds, not hours.

q: Where should a containerized app send its logs, and why?
a: To stdout/stderr — the platform (container runtime/node agent, or journald) captures and ships them. Apps shouldn't manage files/rotation; logs written to files inside a container are ephemeral and invisible to the platform.

q: What single field turns scattered per-service logs into one request's story?
a: A trace/request ID on every log line. With trace_id you can fetch all logs across all services for one request — the concrete form of cross-pillar correlation. Propagating it is what tracing does.

q: What's the typical log aggregation pipeline?
a: app stdout → collector/agent (Fluent Bit, Vector, Promtail) → store + index (Loki or Elasticsearch/OpenSearch) → query/visualize (Grafana or Kibana). Needed because pod logs are ephemeral and numerous.

q: What does distributed tracing show that metrics and logs cannot?
a: The end-to-end path and timing of a single request across all the services it touches — turning 'it's slow somewhere' into 'the payments→bank span took 800ms of 850ms'. It's the pillar built for distributed systems.

q: What are a span and a trace?
a: A span is one unit of work in one service (name, duration, attributes). A trace is a tree of spans for one request — a root span (entry point) with nested child spans — sharing one trace_id.

q: What is context propagation and why is it critical?
a: Passing the trace context (trace ID + parent span) across service calls, typically via the W3C traceparent header, so each service nests its span under the same trace. If a service doesn't forward it, the trace breaks into disconnected pieces.

q: What does OpenTelemetry decouple, and how does the Collector help?
a: It decouples generating telemetry from storing it — instrument once, export anywhere. The OTel Collector receives telemetry, processes it (batch/filter/redact/sample), and exports to one or more backends, centralizing routing and sampling.

q: What is tail-based sampling and why is it popular?
a: Deciding which traces to keep after they complete (in the Collector) — e.g. keep all errored/slow traces plus a sample of normal ones. It retains the traces you actually care about at manageable cost, unlike head sampling which decides blindly up front.

q: What are the four golden signals?
a: Latency (how long requests take — watch p95/p99), Traffic (demand/throughput), Errors (rate of failures), and Saturation (how full the system is). The best default starting set for any service.

q: Define SLI, SLO, and error budget.
a: SLI = a measured indicator (e.g. % of requests <300ms and successful). SLO = the target for it over a window (e.g. 99.9% over 30 days). Error budget = the allowed failure (inverse of SLO; 99.9% → 0.1% budget) — a resource you spend.

q: How does an error budget guide engineering decisions?
a: Plenty of budget left → ship features faster and take risks. Budget exhausted → freeze risky changes and prioritize stability. It makes reliability a managed number and defuses dev-vs-ops debates (100% reliability is the wrong, too-costly target).

q: Why alert on symptoms rather than causes?
a: Symptoms (SLO/error-budget burn rate, user-facing latency/errors) mean real user impact needing a human now. Causes like '85% CPU' are often harmless — paging on them creates alert fatigue, which leads to ignored pages and missed real incidents.

q: What makes a good page (alert)?
a: It must be actionable and urgent — something a human must do right now. If there's nothing to do or it can wait until morning, make it a ticket or dashboard, not a page. Ruthlessly delete non-actionable alerts to fight fatigue.

q: Why shouldn't you scale Prometheus by enlarging its disk?
a: A single Prometheus has no HA and limited retention. Scale by treating it as a collector and using remote_write to a long-term backend (Thanos/Cortex/Mimir) for durable storage, global query, and horizontal scale — plus recording rules to precompute.

q: What is the cardinality trap in Prometheus?
a: Every unique combination of label values is a separate time series. Putting unbounded values (user IDs, request IDs, raw URLs) in labels creates millions of series, exploding memory/cost and crashing the server. Labels are for bounded dimensions only.

q: Where should a high-cardinality identifier like a user ID live?
a: In traces and logs, not in metric labels. Metrics stay low-cardinality (status, method, route template); the specific identifiers belong in the per-request signals you can correlate to.

q: Why must you aggregate histogram buckets before computing a quantile?
a: histogram_quantile must run on summed bucket rates across instances: histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(..._bucket[5m])) by (le)). Averaging pre-computed per-instance p99s is mathematically wrong. Buckets must also straddle your SLO target.

q: What are exemplars and why do they matter for correlation?
a: Exemplars attach a sample trace ID to a metric data point, so a spike on a latency graph links directly to an example slow trace. Combined with trace IDs in logs, you can pivot metric → trace → logs in one click.

q: What is the OpenTelemetry Collector's pipeline, and what is tail sampling?
a: receivers → processors → exporters, decoupling apps from the backend. Tail sampling decides AFTER seeing a whole trace — keep all errors and slow traces, drop boring fast ones — which head sampling (deciding up front) cannot do.

q: Why is error-budget burn-rate alerting better than a static threshold?
a: It pages on what users actually feel — how fast you're consuming the SLO error budget. Multi-window multi-burn-rate rules (a fast window for sharp outages, a slow one for slow burns) catch both without the false pages of 'CPU > 80%'.

q: What do Alertmanager grouping, routing, and inhibition do?
a: Grouping bundles related alerts into one notification; routing sends by severity/team; inhibition suppresses downstream alerts when a parent fires (don't page for 50 services when the cluster is down). Silences mute during maintenance.

q: What does continuous profiling add as a 'fourth pillar'?
a: Metrics/logs/traces show THAT and WHERE it's slow; continuous profiling (pprof, Pyroscope, Parca, eBPF) shows WHICH line of code burns CPU/memory in production — closing the gap from symptom to root cause.
